claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 11 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 11 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Someone who lives with the child is already claiming

Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.**

**How old is the child?**

Please let me know which category applies:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you're claiming for multiple children, please tell me about each of them (their names and ages would be helpful).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I'm looking to claim for my two children.

I have Alex, who's 11 years old, and Blake, who's 15. So they're both under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hi! You're welcome – I'm happy to help you work through this.

Thanks for that information. So we have:
- **Alex** – 11 years old
- **Blake** – 15 years old

Since both children are under 16, they automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit. Let me move to the next step in the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about any work the children might be doing.

**Are both Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

This is usually straightforward for children of their ages, but I need to confirm this for the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work at all. They're both still at school, so they're not doing any paid work.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great, thank you for confirming. Since neither Alex nor Blake are doing any paid work, they both meet this requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving any benefits in their own right?**

Specifically, this means they are not personally claiming benefits like:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

(This is quite rare for children of their ages, but I need to check.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, neither of them receive any benefits in their own name. They're just regular school kids.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you. That's another requirement met for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check about apprenticeships.

**Are Alex and Blake in an apprenticeship?**

Since you mentioned they're both at school, I expect the answer is no – but just to confirm, neither of them is currently doing an apprenticeship programme?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are doing apprenticeships. Like I said, they're both still in school - Alex is only 11 and Blake is 15, so they're just doing their regular school studies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely, that makes sense! With Alex at 11 and Blake at 15, they're both in regular school. That's another box ticked for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check about your residency status.

**Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **any one** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have **pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work
5. You have **pre-settled status** with sufficient resources to support yourself and family
6. You have **pre-settled status** and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have **pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with the right to reside
8. You're a **Crown servant** living abroad (or in certain qualifying countries)

The most common situation is simply that you normally live in the UK. Does that apply to you, or does another option fit your circumstances?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, no - I don't live in the UK. I'm living abroad at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since you're living abroad, I need to check if you might still qualify through the Crown servant route.

**Are you living abroad as a Crown servant?**

Crown servants include people working for the UK government overseas, such as:
- Diplomats
- Members of the Armed Forces stationed abroad
- Civil servants posted overseas

Or are you living in certain qualifying countries with specific arrangements?

If none of these apply to you, unfortunately the residency requirement may not be met.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not a Crown servant or anything like that. I'm just living abroad with my family.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for being upfront about that. Unfortunately, this is where the assessment reaches a conclusion for your situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but based on the information you've provided, **you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit** for either Alex or Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Summary

| Child | Age | Eligible? | Reason |
|-------|-----|-----------|--------|
| **Alex** | 11 | ❌ No | Claimant does not meet UK residency requirements |
| **Blake** | 15 | ❌ No | Claimant does not meet UK residency requirements |

---

**Why not eligible?**

Both Alex and Blake meet all the requirements relating to the child themselves (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship). However, **Child Benefit requires the claimant to normally live in the UK** or meet specific exceptions (such as having settled/pre-settled status or being a Crown servant abroad).

Since you're living abroad and don't fall into any of the qualifying categories, you cannot claim Child Benefit at this time.

---

**What you could do:**

- **If you return to the UK** and become normally resident again, you would likely become eligible to claim for both children (as long as they still meet the child requirements)
- **If your circumstances change** (e.g., you obtain settled status or become a Crown servant), you may become eligible
- You can find more information at: [GOV.UK Child Benefit Eligibility](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/eligibility)

I'm sorry this wasn't the outcome you were hoping for. Is there anything else I can help clarify?

reasonings: Alex (age 11) meets all child-related requirements: under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet UK residency requirements - they do not normally live in the UK, do not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, and are not a Crown servant living abroad. Therefore, the claimant cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake (age 15) meets all child-related requirements: under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. However, the claimant does not meet UK residency requirements - they do not normally live in the UK, do not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, and are not a Crown servant living abroad. Therefore, the claimant cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children (Alex, 11 and Blake, 15) who both meet all the child-related eligibility criteria for Child Benefit. However, the claimant is living abroad and does not qualify under any of the residency pathways: they do not normally live in the UK, do not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, and are not a Crown servant. UK residency (or a qualifying exception) is a fundamental requirement for claiming Child Benefit, so the claimant is ineligible for both children.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.

Blake (age 15) meets all child-related requirements: under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. Therefore, the claimant cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake.
